architecture.summary: This is a theoretical analysis paper examining autoregressive Transformers' expressivity with and without Chain-of-Thought (CoT). The paper proves impossibility results showing bounded-depth Transformers cannot directly solve arithmetic/equation tasks unless model size grows super-polynomially; conversely, it proves by construction that constant-size autoregressive Transformers can solve these tasks by generating CoT derivations step-by-step. The mechanism exploits the recursive nature of autoregressive generation: by looping generated outputs back as inputs, the effective circuit depth becomes proportional to the number of generation steps, enabling solution of problems with high parallel complexity. Information flows from input tokens through Transformer layers; intermediate CoT outputs are regenerated token-by-token, accumulating reasoning steps.

biblio.title: Towards Revealing the Mystery behind Chain of Thought: A Theoretical Perspective

claims.statement: Bounded-depth Transformers are unable to directly produce correct answers for basic arithmetic evaluation tasks unless the model size grows super-polynomially with respect to the input length
Bounded-depth Transformers are unable to directly produce correct answers for linear equation solving tasks unless the model size grows super-polynomially with respect to the input length
Autoregressive Transformers of constant depth O(log n) and constant hidden size suffice to solve arithmetic evaluation tasks by generating CoT derivations using a commonly used math language format
Autoregressive Transformers of constant depth O(log n) and constant hidden size suffice to solve linear equation tasks by generating CoT derivations using a commonly used math language format
LLMs with CoT can solve general Dynamic Programming problems satisfying polynomial approximation assumptions with constant depth and hidden dimension
Bounded-depth Transformers of polynomial size cannot solve Context-Free Grammar Membership Testing without CoT
3-layer Transformers with CoT achieve near-perfect accuracy on all four tasks (Arithmetic, Equation, LIS, and Edit Distance) for all difficulty levels

claims.dataset: Arithmetic task with corrupted CoT steps
claims.statement: 3-layer Transformer model trained on CoT achieves more than 95% accuracy on corrupted arithmetic datasets with 30% of data missing an intermediate CoT step and involving single-token corruption
